denial_letter_text:
Dear Ms. Member,

Aetna has completed its review of the prior authorization request for additional outpatient endocrinology management visits, including hydrocortisone stress-dose education and a repeat ACTH stimulation test, submitted on your behalf. After review, the request is denied.

The annual visit limit for outpatient endocrine-management and self-management education services under this plan has been reached for the current plan year. Additional visits beyond the annual maximum are not a covered benefit for the remainder of the plan year.

The visit-frequency appropriateness criteria applied in this determination are characterized in the ages 18 to 50 population, and under those criteria the number of visits already furnished this plan year is considered sufficient for routine endocrine management. The request for additional supervised visits therefore exceeds the frequency supported by the applicable criteria.

Coverage of outpatient endocrine services is subject to the annual visit maximum and the medical-management frequency criteria described in your plan documents, regardless of the provider or setting in which the services are delivered.

If you disagree with this determination, you may file an internal appeal by contacting Member Services at the number on your identification card within the timeframe stated in your plan documents. You may submit additional clinical information with your appeal request, and a different reviewer will evaluate the appeal.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Aetna Utilization Management
clinical_context: The patient is a 64-year-old woman with primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) confirmed by a morning cortisol of 2.1 mcg/dL and an inadequate ACTH stimulation response, with persistent hyperpigmentation and two emergency department visits this year for adrenal crisis precipitated by intercurrent illness. Her endocrinologist requested continued supervised visits to complete hydrocortisone stress-dosing education and to repeat ACTH stimulation testing, because her glucocorticoid dosing remains unstable and she has already had two crises. The visit-frequency criteria the denial applies are drawn from a population characterized as ages 18 to 50; the Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline for primary adrenal insufficiency supports individualized, ongoing monitoring for older adults with crisis-prone disease and does not endorse a fixed younger-population visit cap for a 64-year-old with documented adrenal crises. The letter cites no patient-specific clinical findings and provides no notice of any right to external independent review.
